coredata.dc:title: AlN and AlP doped graphene quantum dots as novel drug delivery systems for 5-fluorouracil drug: Theoretical studies
coredata.dc:creator: Vatanparast, Morteza
Shariatinia, Zahra
coredata.dc:description: With the help of density functional theory (DFT) calculations, it was attempted to investigate the interaction of 5-fluorouracil (FU) drug and undoped/doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs). To this aim, hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene as well as its BN, BP, AlN and AlP doped (C36X3Y3H18 where XB, Al and YN, P) forms were selected as model GQDs. Results showed that doping of GQDs with AlN and AlP could significantly enhance the tendency of GQDs for adsorption of FU drug. Moreover, Gibbs free energy values indicated that the adsorption of FU drug on the GQDs surfaces was spontaneously occurred. It was found that AlN-FU and AlP-FU complexes had high chemical reactivity, which was crucial for binding of the drug onto the target site. Also, the nature of intermolecular interactions was analyzed based on quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and noncovalent interaction (NCI) analyses. Consequently, the results demonstrated that the AlN and AlP doped GQDs could be used as potential carriers for delivery of FU drug in the nanomedicine domain.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 5-Fluorouracil drug
Graphene quantum dots
Drug delivery
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dipole moment and molecular electrostatic potential

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The financial support of this project by the Research Council of Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), Tehran, Iran is appreciatively acknowledged. The authors are indebted to High Performance Computing Research Center (HPCRC) at Amirkabir University of Technology for providing supercomputers and all of computer services including softwares and hardwares.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The molecular structures of the studied complexes (top view and front view).
The atom numbering of the FU drug and GQDs (XC, B, Al and YC, N, P).
Molecular electrostatic potentials on the 0.001 (electron/Bohr3) electron density isosurfaces of the studied complexes.
The frontier HOMO and LUMO orbitals for FU, GQDs and their complexes.
The density of states (DOS) plots for FU, GQDs and their complexes.
The molecular graphs of the studied complexes obtained by QTAIM analysis.
(Bond critical points represented by violet circles).
Correlation between adsorption energy and sum of electron density (Σρb).
at the BCPs formed between FU drug and GQDs.
The RDG isosurface (0.5 a.u.) for the studied complexes.
The IR spectra for FU, GQDs and their complexes.
TD-DFT calculated UV spectra for FU, GQDs and their complexes.
Selected interatomic distances (Å) of the investigated systems (atom numbering are given in Fig. 2).

Computed adsorption energies (Ead), enthalpy changes (ΔHad), Gibbs free energy changes (ΔGad) and entropy changes (ΔSad) for the adsorption of FU on GQDs in water solution. All the values are in kcal mol−1.

Calculated dipole moment (D, Debye), energies of frontier molecular orbitals (EHOMO, ELUMO, eV), Fermi level (EFL, eV), band gap (Eg, eV), chemical potential (μ, eV), global hardness (η, eV) and global electrophilicity index (ω, eV) of studied systems.

The electron density (ρb), Laplacian of electron density (∇2ρb), kinetic electron energy density (Gb), potential electron energy density (Vb), total electron energy density (Hb) and −Gb/Vb ratio at the bond critical points (BCPs) of the studied complexes. All the values are in au.
The charge density transitions occurred in the UV–vis spectra of the studied complexes.

head.ce:title: AlN and AlP doped graphene quantum dots as novel drug delivery systems for 5-fluorouracil drug: Theoretical studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), P.O. Box: 15875-4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the help of density functional theory (DFT) calculations, it was attempted to investigate the interaction of 5-fluorouracil (FU) drug and undoped/doped graphene quantum dots (GQDs). To this aim, hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene as well as its BN, BP, AlN and AlP doped (C36X3Y3H18 where XB, Al and YN, P) forms were selected as model GQDs. Results showed that doping of GQDs with AlN and AlP could significantly enhance the tendency of GQDs for adsorption of FU drug. Moreover, Gibbs free energy values indicated that the adsorption of FU drug on the GQDs surfaces was spontaneously occurred. It was found that AlN-FU and AlP-FU complexes had high chemical reactivity, which was crucial for binding of the drug onto the target site. Also, the nature of intermolecular interactions was analyzed based on quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and noncovalent interaction (NCI) analyses. Consequently, the results demonstrated that the AlN and AlP doped GQDs could be used as potential carriers for delivery of FU drug in the nanomedicine domain.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene has attracted growing interest due to its unique characteristics such as high electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, mechanical strength, and high electron mobility [1]. In addition to graphene, many efforts have been recently focused on the graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a single or few-layer graphene with tiny sizes of only several nanometers [2,3]. While pristine graphene is a zero band gap material, energy band gaps of GQDs can be engineered by reducing the particles size [4]. GQDs with excellent properties such as unique photoluminescence, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, robust chemical inertness and discrete bandgap are promising candidate for applications in drug delivery, nanoelectronic devices, cellular imaging, disease diagnosis and other applications [5–11].

Recently, the development of novel drug delivery systems (DDSs) with the ability to improve the therapeutic profile and efficacy of therapeutic agents have attracted substantial attention [12]. In conventional drug delivery methods such as oral delivery, etc., the drug concentration in the blood rises quickly, and then declines. Each drug has a therapeutic range above which it is toxic and below which it is ineffective [13,14]. The main aim of an ideal DDS is to send the drug to a specific site and maintain the drug concentration within a desired therapeutic range [12,15,16]. Advances in nanoscience and nanotechnology create new opportunities in the design of new DDSs [17,18]. There are several advantages to the use of nanocarriers as DDSs. Firstly, the small size of nanocarriers allow them to diffuse more quickly than larger particles [19]. Secondly, the nanocarriers have larger surface-to-volume ratio, which provided a larger contact surface between the drug and body in the same drug concentration. Therefore, the nanocarriers can reduce the therapeutic dose of drugs and improve the efficacy of drugs [20]. Finally, the surface of nanocarriers is tunable for different drugs and targeting molecules, thus enable the drug to be directed to the target cells [21,22]. Recently, many different carbon based nanocarriers such as carbon nanotubes [23,24], graphene [25,26], and fullerene [27,28] have been used as DDSs.

5-Fluorouracil (FU) is an effective anticancer drug widely used in the treatment of a variety of cancers such as gastrointestinal, stomach, breast, colon, lung and skin cancers [29,30]. FU is a uracil analogue, which has a fluorine atom at the fifth position [31]. The main challenge of using FU is its short biological half-life, low selectivity and toxic side effects on the bone marrow and gastrointestinal tract [32]. In order to reduce these limitations, nanocarriers have been considered for the controlled release of FU drug to target site [33,34]. Literature precedent shows that mesoporous silica nanoparticles [35], nanogels [36], chitosan nanoparticles [37], cationic cyclodextrin/alginate chitosan nanoflowers [38], magnetic nanoparticles [39] have been used as potential systems for FU delivery.

Heteroatom doping has widely been studied as one of the most feasible methods to modulate the properties of graphene [40]. For instance, B, Al, N, P-doped graphenes have been investigated for different application purposes [41–45]. It would be interesting and important to explore the effects of doping B, Al, N, P heteroatoms in graphene dot on its properties as DDS. The main reasons for the heteroatom doping in a hydrophobic drug carrier such as graphene (CNT or fullerene) is creation of polar functional groups that are able to interact with the functional groups of drug molecule through electrostatic, Hbonding and/or π–π stacking interactions. Such interactions increase the adsorption energy of drug on the carrier surface. Moreover, both of the B and Al elements belong to the group IIIA and both of the N and P elements belong to the group VA of periodic table of elements. Thus, the AlN, AlP pairs are isoelectronic and so do the BN, BP pairs. Consequently, their influences on the electronic and structural properties of graphene would be comparable.

In this study, following our previous researches [46–52], we focus on the use of undoped/doped GQDs as potential carrier candidates for the delivery of FU drug. To this aim, we selected hexa-peri-hexabenzocoronene (HBC, C42H18) as a model of GQDs. In doped GQDs, the three CC bonds of HBC (central ring) were replaced with BN, BP, Al–N and Al–P bonds (C36X3Y3H18 where XB, Al and YN, P), which were denoted as BN, BP, AlN and AlP GQDs, respectively. Properties such as adsorption energy, thermochemical parameters, dipole moments, molecular electrostatic potentials, molecular descriptors, electronic properties, density of states, infrared/ultraviolet-visible (IR/UV–vis) spectra, atoms in molecules and noncovalent interaction index are calculated and compared to each other to obtain more insights into the interaction between FU drug and undoped/doped GQDs.

All the density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed by the Gaussian 09 program package [53]. Geometry optimization of pristine GQDs and their complexes with FU drug were carried out with hybrid B3LYP functional with the 6-31G(d) basis set without any symmetry restrictions in the structures. In addition, Grimme's dispersion corrections (D3) have been included to incorporate the dispersion forces [54,55]. The accuracy and application of this method has been successfully reported in literature [56,57]. All the optimized geometries were confirmed to be minima on the potential energy surface by vibrational frequency analysis at the same level of theory. For all calculations, we used polarizable continuum model (PCM) [58] to account for the solvent effects (water with ε = 78.4). The adsorption energy was calculated as the difference between the energies of optimized complex and the isolated monomers. The enthalpy (ΔHad), Gibbs free energy (ΔGad), entropy (ΔSad) of adsorption for FU drug on the GQDs surface at 298.15 K were obtained according to the following equations:
(1)
Δ X =
X
N G ⋅ ⋅ ⋅ I s o
− (
X
N G
+
X
I s o
)
(2)
Δ S =
( Δ H − Δ G )
298.15
where X = H and G.

The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis [59] was carried out using AIMALL package [60], and the corresponding wave functions were generated at the B3LYP/6-31G(d) level of theory. The Multiwfn [61] and visual molecular dynamics (VMD) programs [62] were used for noncovalent interaction (NCI) analyses. Moreover, density of states diagrams analyses were performed using GaussSum 2.2 program [63].

First of all, we have considered several possible initial configurations to obtain the stable ones. The stable configurations of the studied complexes between FU drug and GQDs are presented in Figs. 1 and S1. Also, the relative energies between different configurations are reported in Table S1. Two most stable configurations have been selected for further investigations. Some of the most important binding distances are given in Table 1 . The numbering of FU drug and GQDs is shown in Fig. 2 . The drug molecule locates above the center of HBC and aligned approximately parallel to the surface of the HBC. All the binding distances between the HBC and drug in the HBC-FU1 and HBC-FU2 complexes are above 3.139 Å. It can be seen that the FU drug tends to be arranged in parallel orientation with respect to the BN GQD surface. The binding distances in the BN-FU1 and BN-FU2 complexes are in the range of 3.087–3.734 and 3.092–3.599 Å, respectively. All the BP, AlN and AlP GQDs have bowl-shaped structures with a bowl depth that depends on the size of the central atoms (P and Al atoms). As can be seen from Table S1, binding at the concave (inside) surface of BP GQD is more stable than the binding at its convex (outside) surface. For the adsorption of FU drug on the BP, AlN and AlP GQDs, the distances are in the range of 2.977–4.722 Å, 1.867–3.658 Å, and 1.927–3.771 Å, respectively. The shortest distance is observed between drug and AlN GQD in AlN-FU1 complex (1.867 Å).

In order to evaluate the interactions between the FU drug and GQDs, adsorption energy (Ead) values were calculated and summarized in Table 2 . As can be seen, the doped GQDs have more negative Ead values compared to undoped GQD (HBC), which indicates that the adsorption ability was increased. The rank order of adsorption of drug on the doped GQDs surfaces is as follows: AlN-FU > AlP-FU > BP-FU > BN-FU. However, significant adsorption takes place on AlN and AlP GQDs surfaces. The adsorption energies for AlN-FU1, AlN-FU2, AlP-FU1 and AlP-FU2 complexes are −47.29, −44.43, −30.61 and −29.14 kcal mol−1, respectively. For comparison, the interaction between benzene and HBC (as a standard complex) is also examined (see Fig. S2). The adsorption energy for HBC-benzene complex is -10.62 kcal mol−1, which is less negative than FU-GQDs complexes.

The adsorption energies of FU drug on different surfaces have been reported for various computational methods. For example, Hazrati et al. [64] reported adsorption energy values of −11.90, −50.13 and −34.19 kcal mol−1 for the adsorption of FU drug on B24N24 fullerene, Al and Si doped B24N24 fullerene in the gas phase, respectively. Shakerzadeh [65], also reported adsorption energies for the adsorption of FU drug on B36 nanosheet in the range of −2.2 to −24.5 kcal mol−1 in the gas phase. Soltani et al. [66] calculated adsorption energy for the adsorption of FU drug on Al-doped BN nanotube of −42.893 kcal mol−1.

The enthalpy (ΔHad), Gibbs free energy (ΔGad) and entropy (ΔSad) of adsorption for FU drug on GQDs are presented in Table 2. The negative values of enthalpy indicate that the adsorption process of FU on doped GQDs is exothermic in nature. For all the complexes, the entropy decreases upon adsorption of FU drug, which can be explained by decrease of the translation degree of freedom. As can be seen from Table 2, the calculated ΔGad values for adsorption of drug on GQDs are negative. Therefore, it is expected that the adsorption of drug on the GQDs surfaces occurs spontaneously. The calculated ΔGad values for AlN-FU, and AlP-FU complexes are the most negative among those of other complexes. Thus, the results confirm that FU drug can effectively loaded on the surface of AlN and AlP GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dipole moment and molecular electrostatic potential
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Table 3 , HBC and BN GQDs are nonpolar, while BP, AlN and AlP GQDs are polar with dipole moments of 5.697, 2.380 and 3.517 Debye, respectively. Also, FU drug is polar with a dipole moment of 5.223 Debye. After the adsorption of FU on the surface of GQDs, the dipole moments of the complexes increase significantly. For instance, the dipole moments in the AlN-FU1 and AlN-FU2 complexes are 18.846 and 13.282 Debye, respectively. These results imply that the adsorption of FU on GQDs enhances the solubility of the complexes, which is a valuable property for drug delivery in biological media.

The molecular electrostatic potentials (MEP) have been used to investigate the distribution of electron density in the studied complexes. The three dimensional MEP maps on the 0.001 electron/Bohr3 contour of the studied complexes are depicted in Fig. 3 . The positive regions of MEP (blue color) indicate the cationic environment, while the negative regions of MEP (red color) show the accumulation of electronic charges. The FU drug has positive potential, which can indicate that the charge transfer is happened from drug to GQDs. As can be seen, significant distribution of positive and negative charges over the FU drug and GQDs causes the charge separation. The asymmetric distribution of the electric charge over the surface of complexes is clearly observable, which is in agreement with the dipole moments data (Table 3).

The frontier molecular orbitals are important parameters in chemical reactions, play major role in the electric and optical properties [67,68]. The energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) is directly related to the ionization potential and the energy of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is directly associated to the electron affinity. The graphical structures of HOMO and LUMO orbitals of all compounds before and after adsorption are depicted in Fig. 4 . As can be seen, the HOMO and LUMO orbitals in FU drug are distributed on the entire parts of the drug molecule so that the HOMO orbital does not exist on the N44H50 bond and the LUMO orbital is not seen on the C45O51 bond. For the HBC and BN GQD, the HOMO and LUMO orbitals are symmetrically distributed on the molecules. The LUMO orbital of BN GQD is located on the carbon atoms, and does not exist on the central ring. For BP, AlN and AlP GQDs, the HOMO orbital is approximately located on the carbon atoms, but LUMO orbital is positioned on the central rings. After adsorption of FU on GQDs, considerable changes are apparent in the HOMO and LUMO orbitals of drug and GQDs. For AlN-FU1, AlN-FU2 and AlP-FU1 complexes, the HOMO orbital is mainly localized on the GQDs and the LUMO orbital is mostly observed on the FU drug. However, for other complexes the HOMO and LUMO orbitals are mainly placed on the GQDs.

To further gain insight into the changes in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap due to interactions, densities of states (DOS) were calculated and shown in Fig. 5 . Also, the energies of frontier molecular orbitals (EHOMO, ELUMO), Fermi level (EFL) and HOMO-LUMO band gap (Eg) of studied systems are also listed in Table 3. The energies of HOMO and LUMO of HBC GQD are −5.43 and −1.84 eV with a HOMO-LUMO energy gap of 3.59 eV, which are in good agreement with the literature values [69–72]. The order of the HOMO-LUMO energy gap of GQDs is as follows: BN (4.06 eV) > AlN (3.61 eV) > BP (3.56 eV) > AlP (3.22 eV). The DOS diagram exhibits that the HOMO-LUMO energy gaps of all complexes, except for the BP-FU2 and AlP-FU2 complex, are decreased after adsorption of FU on GQDs. The change in HOMO-LUMO energy gaps is relatively small for HBC-FU, BN-FU and BP-FU complexes whereas significantly large changes are observed for AlN-FU and AlP-FU complexes. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap of AlN GQD is estimated to be 3.61 eV and a significant reduction in band gap is observed for AlN-FU1 complex (Eg = 2.08 eV). Thus, the HOMO-LUMO energy gap is decreased by about 42.4%, which will alter the fluorescence emission of the complex and can help to trace the drug trajectory. As displayed in Fig. 5, the change in the shape of DOS plots compared to the pristine GQDs is relatively small for HBC-FU, BN-FU and BP-FU complexes whereas large changes are observed for AlN-FU and AlP-FU complexes.

To gain further insights into the weak interactions in the adsorption of FU drug on the surface of GQDs, the NCI index was used. In this method, noncovalent interactions are identified by the peaks that appear in the reduced density gradient (RDG) at low densities [77]. Based on second eigenvalue of the electron density Hessian matrix (λ2) and electron density (ρ), sign(λ2)ρ < 0 represented an attractive interaction (such as hydrogen bonding); sign(λ2)ρ > 0 implied a repulsion interaction (such as steric repulsion); sign(λ2)ρ ≈ 0 featured the van der Wals (vdW) interaction. The RDG isosurface can be applied to visualization of weak interactions on a blue–green–red scale. The blue, green and red regions represent the attractive, vdW and repulsive interactions, respectively. The RDG isosurface (0.5 a.u.) for studied complexes is shown in Fig. 8 . The regions between FU drug and HBC, BN and BP GQDs are shown as green, implying that the vdW interactions. The blue regions between FU drug and the AlN and AlP GQDs arises as a result of strong attractive interaction. Due to the large size of the central ring atoms (P and Al atoms) in AlP GQDs, strong repulsion interactions were observed in AlP-FU1 and AlP-FU2 complexes.

The calculated IR spectra of FU, GQDs and their complexes in water solution are presented in Fig. 9 . For FU drug, the peaks appeared at 3633 and 3595 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of N46H and N44H bonds, respectively. The bands at around 1806 and 1763 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C45O and C43O bonds. The bands at around 1267 cm−1 in FU drug are assigned to CF stretching mode. These data are in agreement with published FT-IR spectra for FU drug [78]. After adsorption of FU drug on HBC and BN GQDs, frequencies of important vibrational modes associated with drug do not shift significantly. The N44H stretching band shows red shift to 3455, 3565 and 3252 cm−1 for AlP-FU2, AlN-FU1, and AlN-FU2 complexes, respectively. The N46H stretching band of FU drug in BP-FU1, AlP-FU1 and AlN-FU1 complexes exhibited significant red shift to 3596, 3595 and 3593 cm−1, respectively. The IR peak due to the C45O stretching vibration of the drug molecule is shifted to 1697 and 1688 cm−1 for AlN-FU2, and AlP-FU2 complexes, respectively. Moreover, the C43O stretching band shows red shift to 1654 and 1657 cm−1 for AlN-FU1 and AlP-FU1 complexes, respectively. These red shifts are due to the presence of interactions between the FU drug and the GQDs surfaces.

The UV–vis spectra of FU drug, GQDs and complexes computed in water solution are presented in Fig. 10 . Also, Table 5 lists the most important transitions (highest oscillator strengths). Five peaks exist in the spectrum of FU drug which are located at 248.72, 199.11, 161.84, 134.27 and 115.00 nm with oscillator strengths of 0.1509, 0.1267, 0.4106, 0.1833 and 0.0869, respectively. The peak observed at 161.84 nm (maximum oscillator strength) is dominantly (90%) due to the H-2 → L + 1 transition. After the adsorption of FU drug on the surface of GQDs, absorption bands of FU disappeared. It is observed in Fig. 10 that the shapes of UV–vis peaks in GQD-FU complexes are similar to that of their corresponding GQDs. Similar results were achieved in our previous study on the adsorption of hydrocarbons over the CNT surface for which the UV–vis spectra of complexes were completely similar to that of the CNT [79]. It can be seen that the intensities of absorption peaks of GQDs are decreased after adsorption of FU drug. The spectrum of AlN GQD reveals two peaks at 373.79 and 275.33 nm with oscillator strengths of 0.2223 and 0.1047, respectively. The peak detected at 373.79 nm is associated with two transitions from HOMO-1 to LUMO + 2 (44%) and HOMO to LUMO + 1 (48%). The absorption peaks of AlN GQD show red shift to 379.27 and 290.01 nm for AlN-FU1 complex. For other GQDs, the absorption peaks positions remain approximately constant after adsorption process.

The adsorption of FU drug on pristine and doped GQDs, has been investigated theoretically using the DFT-D3 method. The comparison between the adsorption energy values reveals that doping of GQDs with AlN and AlP atoms can significantly enhance the tendency of GQDs for adsorption of FU drug. The rank order of adsorption of drug on the doped GQDs surfaces is as follows: AlN-FU > AlP-FU > BP-FU > BN-FU. The most negative adsorption energy was observed for the AlN-FU1 complex (−47.29 kcal mol−1). Therefore, the FU drug can be easily attached onto the surface of AlN and AlP GQDs, which can facilitate drug loading efficiency. The negative values of enthalpy indicate that the adsorption process of FU on doped GQDs is exothermic in nature. Moreover, Gibbs free energy values indicate that the adsorption of FU drug on the GQDs surfaces occurs spontaneously. The AlN-FU and AlP-FU complexes illustrated smallest HOMO-LUMO energy gaps, chemical potentials and global hardnesses as well as the greatest electrophilicity index. These results indicate that the AlN-FU and AlP-FU complexes have high chemical reactivity, which is crucial for binding of FU drug onto the target site. Also, the nature of intermolecular interactions is investigated by QTAIM and NCI analyses. The results indicate that there is a linear relationship between the adsorption energy and the sum of electron density values at the BCPs formed between FU drug and GQDs. The results obtained from the present investigation confirmed that the AlN and AlP doped GQDs could be used as potential carriers for drug delivery application, although further in vivo studies are needed to confirm these findings.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of this project by the Research Council of Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), Tehran, Iran is appreciatively acknowledged. The authors are indebted to High Performance Computing Research Center (HPCRC) at Amirkabir University of Technology for providing supercomputers and all of computer services including softwares and hardwares.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfluchem.2018.04.003.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene and graphene oxide: synthesis, properties, and applications
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
From nanographene and graphene nanoribbons to graphene sheets: chemical synthesis
Functionalized quantum dots for biosensing and bioimaging and concerns on toxicity

Improvement of photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots with a biocompatible photochemical reduction pathway and its bioimaging application
Luminscent graphene quantum dots for organic photovoltaic devices
Graphene quantum dots as universal fluorophores and their use in revealing regulated trafficking of insulin receptors in adipocytes
Spotlighting graphene quantum dots and beyond: synthesis, properties and sensing applications
A novel chitosan-polyethylene oxide nanofibrous mat designed for controlled co-release of hydrocortisone and imipenem/cilastatin drugs
Mechanical properties and antibacterial activities of novel nanobiocomposite films of chitosan and starch
Synthesis and antibacterial activities of novel nanocomposite films of chitosan/phosphoramide/Fe3O4 NPs
Antibacterial activities of novel nanocomposite biofilms of chitosan/phosphoramide/Ag NPs

Antibacterial electrospun chitosan−polyethylene oxidenanocomposite mats containing bioactive silver nanoparticles
Chitosan-based hydrogels: preparation, properties and applications
Nanoparticle-based sorting of circulating tumor cells by epithelial antigen expression during disease progression in an animal model
New progress and prospects: the application of nanogel in drug delivery
Extraction and encapsulation of prodigiosin in chitosan microspheres for targeted drug delivery
A review of drug release mechanisms from nanocarrier systems
Filled carbon nanotubes in biomedical imaging and drug delivery
Characterization of different carbon nanotubes for the development of a mucoadhesive drug delivery system for intravesical treatment of bladder cancer
Stimuli responsive drug delivery systems based on nano-graphene for cancer therapy
Graphene and graphene oxide as new nanocarriers for drug delivery applications
A tumoral acidic pH-responsive drug delivery system based on a novel photosensitizer (fullerene) for in vitro and in vivo chemo-photodynamic therapy
C60-fullerenes for delivery of docetaxel to breast cancer cells: a promising approach for enhanced efficacy and better pharmacokinetic profile
5-fluorouracil: mechanisms of action and clinical strategies
Synthesis, characterization and in vitro biological assays of A silver(I) complex with 5-fluorouracil: a strategy to overcome multidrug resistant tumor cells
Competitive binding of anticancer drugs 5-fluorouracil and cyclophosphamide with serum albumin: calorimetric insights
Novel strategies to improve the anticancer action of 5-fluorouracil by using drug delivery systems

Solvent effects in the development of a drug delivery system for 5-fluorouracil using magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles
Nanogels fabricated by lysozyme and sodium carboxymethyl cellulose for 5-fluorouracil controlled release
The effect of the molecular weight of chitosan nanoparticles and its application on drug delivery
Cationic cyclodextrin/alginate chitosan nanoflowers as 5-fluorouracil drug delivery system
Silica coated LSMO magnetic nanoparticles for the pH-Responsive delivery of 5-Fluorouracil anticancer drug
Atomic layers of hybridized boron nitride and graphene domains
Nitrogen-doped graphene as efficient metal-free electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction in fuel cells
Nitrogen-doped graphene and its application in electrochemical biosensing
B, N-and P, N-doped graphene as highly active catalysts for oxygen reduction reactions in acidic media
Al-doped graphene as modified nanostructure sensor for some ether molecules: Ab-initio study
A DFT study on the central-ring doped HBC nanographenes
Phosphate functionalized (4,4)-armchair CNTs as novel drug delivery systems for alendronate and etidronate anti-osteoporosis drugs
DFT computations on the hydrogen bonding interactions between methacrylic acid-trimethylolpropane trimethacrylate copolymers and letrozole as drug delivery systems
